calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Feb-26-2024 Vacated

PLAINTIFF AND CROSS-DEFENDANT RES SYSTEM 3, LLC TO TRANSFER VENUE PURSUANT TO CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 394
Off Calendar Jun-02-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF AND CROSS-DEFENDANT RES SYSTEM 3, LLC TO TRANSFER VENUE PURSUANT TO CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 394.

Plaintiff RES System 3, LLC's ("RES") motion to transfer venue is GRANTED. The proceedings are ordered transferred to Alameda County.

California Civil Procedure section 394 contains several provisions relating to the place of trials in actions by or against counties and cities, and includes both complaints and cross complaints. (Newman v. Sonoma County (1961) 56 Cal.2d 625, 626; Metropolitan Transit System v. Sup. Ct. (2007) 153 Cal.App.4th 293, 302.) This section permits a non-resident defendant to have venue transferred to a neutral county. (Metropolitan Transit System, 153 Cal.App.4th at 295.) "A corporation is a "resident" only if its activities in the county are substantial enough that it can reasonably be viewed as being intimately identified with the affairs or closely associated with the people of the community." (Edmon & Karnow, CALIFORNIA PRACTICE GUIDE: CIVIL PROCEDURE BEFORE TRIAL 3:548.1 (Rutter 2022).)

Here, Defendant City and County of San Francisco ("the City") filed a cross-complaint against RES. RES is a limited liability corporation formed in Delaware, with its principal place of business in Denver, Colorado. (Settle Decl., 5.) RES has only one office in California, located in Sacramento. (Id. at 6.) This litigation arises out of the work contract for a project located in Stanislaus County. (Id. at 7.) RES is not closely associated with San Francisco for section 394 purposes. Thus, RES is a non-resident.

The City argues that the parties' venue-selection clause takes priority over Code of Civil Procedure section 394. Not so. Because of the public purpose underlying section 394, the rights conferred by section 394 cannot be waived by advance agreement of the parties. (Arntz Builders v. Superior Court (2004) 122 Cal.App.4th 1195, 1207.)

The selection of a receiving court may be decided by stipulation of the parties or the court. Considering that one party is the City and County of San Francisco and the other only has one office in Sacramento, the court is inclined to select Alameda County unless the parties agree to a new county before or at the hearing.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

MOTION TO TRANSFER
Off Calendar May-12-2023 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-30-2023 Set For Trial On Mar-11-24 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-06-2023 Continued To Apr-12-23 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-17-2023 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT

No appearance.

The court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT as follows:

Defendant's demurrer to amended complaint is overruled.

The court liberally construes the amended complaint and accepts plaintiff's allegations as true on demurrer. (See CCP 452; Berger v. Varum (2019) 35 Cal.App.5th 1013, 1018 ["We accept as true all well-pleaded allegations in the operative complaint"].)

Here, the amended complaint alleges ultimate facts that the period of time to submit a government claim was tolled during the contractual administrative process and the tolling ended on December 7, 2021. (Amended Complaint, pars. 8, 18.) The parties' agreement, which is attached to the amended complaint, provides: "the timely submittal of a complete and proper Contract Claim under the administrative procedure specified in this Article 13 shall operate to toll Design- Builder's compliance with the Government Code Claim requirements under California Government Code section 900, et seq., and San Francisco Administrative Code Chapter 10 until the City issues a final administrative determination per subparagraph 13.03, above." (Amended Complaint, Ex. 2 [Contract, General Conditions, Art. 13.04, p. 74].)

The Government Claims Act sets forth a one-year deadline for the submission of a claim under the circumstances of this case. (See Cal. Gov. Code 911.2(a), 911.4 (b).) Based on plaintiff's allegations, plaintiff submitted the government claim on February 3, 2022, and it is therefore timely. (Amended Complaint, Ex. 1 [government claim].) The court declines Defendant's invitation to deem established as a fact that "Substantial Completion" occurred on March 4, 2021. "Substantial Completion" is a defined term amounting to an equitable doctrine akin to substantial performance, not a straightforward historical fact. Further, to be sure, the parties disagree about many material facts (e.g., effect of the DRA process, the adequacy of the claim). The court, however, has, as it must, accepted plaintiff's allegations as true for purposes of demurrer.

Order signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Proceedings not reported. (302/JMQ)
